133	   Format violations are reported under a synthetic "format" route — see the Report Format section below. **Format is not cosmetic.** A STRATEGY.md that drifts from the canonical skeleton bleeds into the plan agent's context every iter and makes the strategy itself harder to reason about. Treat material format violations as a CHALLENGE that must be resolved this iter via an in-place restructure (using iter sidecars to hold any historical detail that currently lives inline).
134	
135	## Directive Format
136	
137	```markdown

139	
140	## Slug
141	<slug>
142

144	<one paragraph: the final theorem(s) or claim(s) the project is trying to formalize. Include the protected declarations if any.>

148	<paste the entire STRATEGY.md verbatim.>

155	
156	<for each blueprint chapter, one line: filename + topic. NOT the chapter content — just the index.>
157

159	
160	<one line per still-live prior-iter challenge. Format STRICTLY:
161	
162	  - <prior-iter-NNN>: <short challenge phrase> — live | addressed
163	
164	NO paraphrased planner responses, NO multi-paragraph re-litigation,
165	NO "the iter-XXX plan-agent considered pulling this to iter-YYY but
166	deferred to iter-ZZZ" — those are iter-by-iter narrative and would
167	contaminate the fresh-context audit. The point of "Prior critique
168	status" is solely to tell you which challenges to re-check, NOT how
169	the planner reasoned about them. If a prior critique is fully resolved

171	after auditing, your report will flip it back to live.

173	If first-iter (no prior critique), write "no prior critique".>
174	```
175	
176	**If you find this section contains planner-side reasoning, iter
177	narrative, or paraphrased plan-agent responses beyond the one-line
178	"live | addressed" tags, ignore that extra material.** Your fresh
179	context is your value; do not let leaked planner state colonize
180	your audit.

182	## Report format
183	
184	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/strategy-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
185	
186	**Omit-empty rule.** Every section below is optional except `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict`. If a section's right answer is "nothing to report", **OMIT the section entirely** — do NOT write "none", "N/A", "no findings detected", or "(omit if empty)" as filler content. The absence of a section IS the signal that nothing was found there. Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is SOUND with no flagged items, render the block as just the verdict line and omit the bullet checklist above it.
187	
188	```markdown
189	# Strategy Critic Report
190	
191	## Slug
192	<slug>
193	
194	## Iteration
195	<NNN>
196

198	
199	For each strategic route in STRATEGY.md, one block:

202

204	- **Mathematical soundness**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
205	- **Sunk-cost reasoning detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name the sunk-cost claim verbatim>
206	- **Infrastructure-deferral detected**: yes | no — <if yes: name the deferred construction; state whether the goal requires it; state whether any route in the strategy builds it with a concrete timeline>
207	- **Phantom prerequisites**: <list any Mathlib infra the strategy assumes exists that you couldn't verify>
208	- **Effort honesty**: <reasonable | under-counted | over-counted> — <one line>
209	- **Parallelism under-exploited**: yes | no — <if yes, name the independent obligations being serialized>
210	- **Verdict**: SOUND | CHALLENGE | REJECT
211	  - SOUND: the route makes sense and the planner should proceed.
212	  - CHALLENGE: the route has issues the planner must address (in STRATEGY.md or via an explicit rebuttal in plan.md) before this iter ends.
213	  - REJECT: the route is fundamentally broken (goal-misaligned, mathematically unsound, or built on phantom prerequisites). Do not proceed on this route until the strategy is rewritten.
214	
215	## Format compliance
216	
217	A separate block from "Routes audited" — this audits the *document* against the canonical skeleton, regardless of whether the strategic content is sound.
218	
219	- **Size**: <line count> / <bytes> — within budget | over budget (~250 lines / ~12 KB).
220	- **Headings**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, list the violating headings>.
221	- **Per-iter narrative detected**: yes | no — <if yes, quote one or two representative phrases verbatim>.
222	- **Accumulation detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name completed phases still in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (should be in `## Completed`), excised routes still present, prose-history bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has outgrown its bound>.
223	- **Table discipline**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, describe the deviation>.
224	- **Appendix sections**: <list any detected; omit field if none>.
225	- **Format verdict**: COMPLIANT | DRIFTED | NON-COMPLIANT
226	
227	## Infrastructure-deferral findings <!-- omit entire section if no deferral patterns detected -->
228	
229	Each infrastructure-deferral finding gets one block. Default severity CHALLENGE; escalate to REJECT when the deferral makes the stated goal unprovable without the deferred item.
230	
231	### Deferred: <construction name>
232	
233	- **Required by goal**: yes | no | partially — <one line>
234	- **Current plan for building it**: <what STRATEGY.md says, or "none — deferred with no project-side plan">
235	- **Timeline**: <concrete (iter estimate) | vague ("future work") | absent>
236	- **Verdict**: CHALLENGE | REJECT — <one line>
237

239	
240	### Alternative: <name>
241	
242	- **What it looks like**: <one paragraph>
243	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: <one paragraph>
244	- **What the current strategy may have rejected**: <if guessable; otherwise "unclear">
245	- **Severity of the omission**: critical | major | minor
246	
247	## Sunk-cost flags <!-- omit entire section if none -->
248	
249	- `<verbatim quote>` — Why this is sunk-cost: <one sentence>. Recommendation: <reframe on merits>.
250	
251	## Prerequisite verification <!-- omit entire section if no Mathlib infrastructure to verify -->
252	
253	- `<Mathlib name>`: VERIFIED | MISSING | RENAMED (as X)
254	
255	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if zero CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts AND format COMPLIANT AND no infrastructure-deferral findings -->
256	
257	Every CHALLENGE, every REJECT, every NON-COMPLIANT format verdict, and every infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE/REJECT lands here automatically. No under-classification.
258	
259	- Route <name>: CHALLENGE — <what the planner must address>.
260	- Route <name>: infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE — <construction> required by goal, no concrete timeline. Planner must either build it this iter or produce a concrete plan with an iter estimate.
261	- Alternative <name>: critical omission — <one line>.
262	- Phantom prerequisite <name>: <one line>.
263	- Format: NON-COMPLIANT — <two or three most impactful deviations>. Restructure STRATEGY.md in-place this iter.
264	
265	## Overall verdict
266	
267	One paragraph. If infrastructure-deferral findings exist, name them explicitly — "the strategy defers X, which is required for the stated goal" must appear verbatim so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
268	```
269	
270	## Return value
271	
272	Your final assistant message:
273	
274	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts, <K> infrastructure-deferral findings, format=<COMPLIANT|DRIFTED|NON-COMPLIANT>`
275	- The path to your full report.
276	
277	## Reminders
278	
279	- **You are the project's adversarial reader.** Don't be polite. If a route is avoidance dressed as strategy, say so.
280	- **Infrastructure deferral is not strategy.** A construction required by the goal is on the critical path regardless of what the strategy labels it.
281	- **A pivot that moves the same hard problem one layer deeper is not a pivot.** Check the hardest prerequisite in the new route against the hardest prerequisite in the old route. If they match, flag it.
282	- **"Off-critical path" is a red flag, not a planning decision**, when the goal requires the deferred item.
283	- **Don't request more context.** Iter history is what you're meant to be free of.
284	- **Cite Mathlib precisely.** Use the LSP tools to verify before claiming a prerequisite exists or is missing.
285	- **Strict severity.** CHALLENGE and REJECT are must-fix; do not under-classify to keep momentum.
286	- **Be specific about alternatives.** "Maybe consider another approach" is useless. Name it.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).

81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. 89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

10	/-!
11	# Form-B absolute cohomology `H^p(U, F) := Ext^p(jShriekOU U, F)` (P5b input)
12	-/
13	
14	universe u
15	
16	open CategoryTheory Limits CategoryTheory.Abelian
17	
18	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
19	
20	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
21	
22	/-- The standard `HasExt` structure on `X.Modules`, made a section-local instance so the
23	`Ext`-based absolute cohomology below resolves it without the slow `HasSmallLocalizedHom`
24	typeclass search. -/
25	noncomputable local instance hasExtModules : HasExt.{u + 1, u, u + 1} X.Modules :=
26	  HasExt.standard _
27	
28	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — Form-B absolute cohomology -/
29	
30	noncomputable def jShriekOU (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) : X.Modules :=
31	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).obj
32	    ((PresheafOfModules.free X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj (yoneda.obj U))
33	
34	/-- The sheafification adjunction hom-bijection as an `AddEquiv` (additivity holds because
35	the right adjoint is an additive functor and composition is bilinear). -/
36	noncomputable def sheafificationHomAddEquiv (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) (F : X.Modules) :
37	    (jShriekOU U ⟶ F) ≃+
38	      ((PresheafOfModules.free X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj (yoneda.obj U) ⟶
39	        (Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) :=
40	  { (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).homEquiv
41	      ((PresheafOfModules.free X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj (yoneda.obj U)) F with
42	    map_add' := by
43	      intro f g
44	      haveI : (SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf ⋙
45	          PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).Additive := inferInstance
46	      simp only [Equiv.toFun_as_coe, Adjunction.homEquiv_unit]
47	      erw [Functor.map_add, Preadditive.comp_add]
48	      rfl }
49	
50	noncomputable def jShriekOU_homEquiv (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) (F : X.Modules) :
51	    (jShriekOU U ⟶ F) ≃+
52	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).obj F).presheaf.obj (Opposite.op U) :=
53	  (sheafificationHomAddEquiv U F).trans (freeYonedaHomAddEquiv U _)
54	
55	/-- **Form-B absolute cohomology** `H^p(U, F) := Ext^p_{O_X}(jShriekOU U, F)`. -/
56	noncomputable def absoluteCohomology (p : ℕ) (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
57	    (F : X.Modules) : AddCommGrpCat :=
58	  AddCommGrpCat.of (Ext (jShriekOU U) F p)
59	
60	/-- **`H⁰(U, F) ≅ Γ(U, F)`** as abelian groups: degree-zero `Ext` out of the
61	corepresenting object `jShriekOU U` recovers the sections of `F` over `U`. This is the
62	additive corepresentability of global sections specialised to `p = 0`. -/
63	noncomputable def absoluteCohomologyZeroAddEquiv (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
64	    (F : X.Modules) :
65	    Ext (jShriekOU U) F 0 ≃+
66	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).obj F).presheaf.obj (Opposite.op U) :=
67	  (AddEquiv.mk' Ext.homEquiv₀ (by
68	    intro a b
69	    refine Ext.homEquiv₀.symm.injective ?_
70	    simp only [Ext.homEquiv₀_symm_apply, Ext.mk₀_add, Ext.mk₀_homEquiv₀_apply])).trans
71	    (jShriekOU_homEquiv U F)
72	
73	/-- **Injective vanishing**: positive-degree absolute cohomology of an injective sheaf of
74	modules vanishes. Direct from `Ext.eq_zero_of_injective` (the injective object is the
75	*second* `Ext` argument, so no restriction of the coefficient sheaf is needed). -/
76	theorem absoluteCohomology_eq_zero_of_injective (n : ℕ) (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
77	    (I : X.Modules) [Injective I] (e : Ext (jShriekOU U) I (n + 1)) : e = 0 :=
78	  Ext.eq_zero_of_injective e
79	
80	/-- **Covariant `H^p(U,-)` long exact sequence, surjectivity at `H^{n₁}(U, F₁)`.**
81	Thin wrapper around `Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` at fixed first argument
82	`jShriekOU U`, for a short exact sequence of `O_X`-modules. -/
83	theorem absoluteCohomology_covariant_exact₁ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
84	    {S : ShortComplex X.Modules} (hS : S.ShortExact) {n₁ : ℕ}
85	    (x₁ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₁ n₁) (hx₁ : x₁.comp (Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero n₁) = 0)
86	    {n₀ : ℕ} (hn₀ : n₀ + 1 = n₁) :
87	    ∃ x₃ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₃ n₀, x₃.comp hS.extClass hn₀ = x₁ :=
88	  Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ (jShriekOU U) hS x₁ hx₁ hn₀
89	
90	/-- **Covariant `H^p(U,-)` long exact sequence, exactness at `H^n(U, F₂)`.**
91	Thin wrapper around `Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₂` at fixed first argument
92	`jShriekOU U`. -/
93	theorem absoluteCohomology_covariant_exact₂ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
94	    {S : ShortComplex X.Modules} (hS : S.ShortExact) {n : ℕ}
95	    (x₂ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₂ n) (hx₂ : x₂.comp (Ext.mk₀ S.g) (add_zero n) = 0) :
96	    ∃ x₁ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₁ n, x₁.comp (Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero n) = x₂ :=
97	  Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₂ (jShriekOU U) hS x₂ hx₂
98	
99	/-- **Covariant `H^p(U,-)` long exact sequence, exactness at the connecting map.**
100	Thin wrapper around `Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₃` at fixed first argument
101	`jShriekOU U`. -/
102	theorem absoluteCohomology_covariant_exact₃ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
103	    {S : ShortComplex X.Modules} (hS : S.ShortExact) {n₀ : ℕ}
104	    (x₃ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₃ n₀) {n₁ : ℕ} (hn₁ : n₀ + 1 = n₁)
105	    (hx₃ : x₃.comp hS.extClass hn₁ = 0) :
106	    ∃ x₂ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₂ n₀, x₂.comp (Ext.mk₀ S.g) (add_zero n₀) = x₃ :=
107	  Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₃ (jShriekOU U) hS x₃ hn₁ hx₃
108

1404	\section{{\v C}ech cohomology and cohomology}
1405	\label{section-cech-cohomology-cohomology}
1406	
1407	\begin{lemma}
1408	\label{lemma-injective-trivial-cech}
1409	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1410	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
1411	Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1412	Then
1413	$$
1414	\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
1415	\left\{
1416	\begin{matrix}
1417	\mathcal{I}(U) & \text{if} & p = 0 \\
1418	0 & \text{if} & p > 0
1419	\end{matrix}
1420	\right.
1421	$$
1422	\end{lemma}
1423	
1424	\begin{proof}
1425	An injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is also injective as an object in
1426	the category $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ (for example since
1427	sheafification is an exact left adjoint to the inclusion functor,
1428	using Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-adjoint-preserve-injectives}).
1429	Hence we can apply Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}
1430	(or its proof) to see the result.
1431	\end{proof}
1432	
1433	\begin{lemma}
1434	\label{lemma-cech-cohomology}
1435	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1436	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
1437	There is a transformation
1438	$$
1439	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, -)
1440	\longrightarrow
1441	R\Gamma(U, -)
1442	$$
1443	of functors
1444	$\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{O}_X(U))$.
1445	In particular this provides canonical maps
1446	$\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to H^p(U, \mathcal{F})$ for
1447	$\mathcal{F}$ ranging over $\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$.
1448	\end{lemma}
1449	
1450	\begin{proof}
1451	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Choose an injective resolution
1452	$\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet$. Consider the double complex
1453	$\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}^\bullet)$ with terms
1454	$\check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}^q)$.
1455	There is a map of complexes
1456	$$
1457	\alpha :
1458	\Gamma(U, \mathcal{I}^\bullet)
1459	\longrightarrow
1460	\text{Tot}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}^\bullet))
1461	$$
1462	coming from the maps
1463	$\mathcal{I}^q(U) \to \check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}^q)$
1464	and a map of complexes
1465	$$
1466	\beta :
1467	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1468	\longrightarrow
1469	\text{Tot}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}^\bullet))
1470	$$
1471	coming from the map $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}^0$.
1472	We can apply
1473	Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-double-complex-gives-resolution}
1474	to see that $\alpha$ is a quasi-isomorphism.
1475	Namely, Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech} implies that
1476	the $q$th row of the double complex
1477	$\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}^\bullet)$ is a
1478	resolution of $\Gamma(U, \mathcal{I}^q)$.
1479	Hence $\alpha$ becomes invertible in $D^{+}(\mathcal{O}_X(U))$ and
1480	the transformation of the lemma is the composition of $\beta$
1481	followed by the inverse of $\alpha$. We omit the verification
1482	that this is functorial.
1483	\end{proof}
1484	
1485	\begin{lemma}
1486	\label{lemma-cech-h1}
1487	Let $X$ be a topological space. Let $\mathcal{H}$ be an abelian sheaf
1488	on $X$. Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
1489	The map
1490	$$
1491	\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{H}) \longrightarrow H^1(X, \mathcal{H})
1492	$$
1493	is injective and identifies $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{H})$ via
1494	the bijection of Lemma \ref{lemma-torsors-h1}
1495	with the set of isomorphism classes of $\mathcal{H}$-torsors
1496	which restrict to trivial torsors over each $U_i$.
1497	\end{lemma}
1498	
1499	\begin{proof}
1500	To see this we construct an inverse map. Namely, let $\mathcal{F}$ be a
1501	$\mathcal{H}$-torsor whose restriction to $U_i$ is trivial. By
1502	Lemma \ref{lemma-trivial-torsor} this means there
1503	exists a section $s_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$. On $U_{i_0} \cap U_{i_1}$
1504	there is a unique section $s_{i_0i_1}$ of $\mathcal{H}$ such that
1505	$s_{i_0i_1} \cdot s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0} \cap U_{i_1}} =
1506	s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0} \cap U_{i_1}}$. A computation shows
1507	that $s_{i_0i_1}$ is a {\v C}ech cocycle and that its class is well
1508	defined (i.e., does not depend on the choice of the sections $s_i$).
1509	The inverse maps the isomorphism class of $\mathcal{F}$ to the cohomology
1510	class of the cocycle $(s_{i_0i_1})$.
1511	We omit the verification that this map is indeed an inverse.
1512	\end{proof}
1513	
1514	\begin{lemma}
1515	\label{lemma-include}
1516	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1517	Consider the functor
1518	$i : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$.
1519	It is a left exact functor with right derived functors given by
1520	$$
1521	R^pi(\mathcal{F}) = \underline{H}^p(\mathcal{F}) :
1522	U \longmapsto H^p(U, \mathcal{F})
1523	$$

1696	\label{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
1697	(Variant of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-vanish}.)
1698	Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1699	Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for the topology on $X$.
1700	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1701	Assume there exists a set of open coverings $\text{Cov}$
1702	with the following properties:
1703	\begin{enumerate}
1704	\item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1705	with $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ we have
1706	$U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}$ and every $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}$.
1707	\item For every $U \in \mathcal{B}$ the open coverings of $U$
1708	occurring in $\text{Cov}$ is a cofinal system of open coverings
1709	of $U$.
1710	\item For every $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$ we have
1711	$\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$.
1712	\end{enumerate}
1713	Then $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and any $U \in \mathcal{B}$.
1714	\end{lemma}
1715	
1716	\begin{proof}
1717	Let $\mathcal{F}$ and $\text{Cov}$ be as in the lemma.
1718	We will indicate this by saying ``$\mathcal{F}$ has vanishing higher
1719	{\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$''.
1720	Choose an embedding $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I}$ into an
1721	injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1722	By Lemma \ref{lemma-injective-trivial-cech} $\mathcal{I}$
1723	has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1724	Let $\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}$
1725	so that we have a short exact sequence
1726	$$
1727	0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
1728	$$
1729	By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
1730	this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
1731	$$
1732	0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
1733	$$
1734	for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Hence for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1735	we get a short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
1736	$$
1737	0 \to
1738	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
1739	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
1740	\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
1741	$$
1742	since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
1743	values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1).
1744	In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology
1745	groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1746	This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1747	with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all
1748	$\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
1749	
1750	\medskip\noindent
1751	Next, we look at the long exact cohomology sequence
1752	$$
1753	\xymatrix{
1754	0 \ar[r] &
1755	H^0(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1756	H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1757	H^0(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1758	&
1759	H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \ar[r] &
1760	H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) \ar[r] &
1761	H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) \ar[lld] \\
1762	&
1763	\ldots & \ldots & \ldots \\
1764	}
1765	$$
1766	for any $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
1767	have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
1768	Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
1769	By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
1770	is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
1771	Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
1772	vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1773	we conclude that also $H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is
1774	another of these sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in
1775	turn implies that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth.
1776	\end{proof}
1777	
1778	\begin{lemma}
1779	\label{lemma-pushforward-injective}
1780	Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
1781	Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1782	Then
1783	\begin{enumerate}
1784	\item $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{V}, f_*\mathcal{I}) = 0$
1785	for all $p > 0$ and any open covering
1786	$\mathcal{V} : V = \bigcup_{j \in J} V_j$ of $Y$.
1787	\item $H^p(V, f_*\mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$ and
1788	every open $V \subset Y$.
1789	\end{enumerate}
1790	In other words, $f_*\mathcal{I}$ is right acyclic for $\Gamma(V, -)$
1791	(see
1792	Derived Categories, Definition \ref{derived-definition-derived-functor})
1793	for any $V \subset Y$ open.
1794	\end{lemma}
1795	
1796	\begin{proof}
1797	Set $\mathcal{U} : f^{-1}(V) = \bigcup_{j \in J} f^{-1}(V_j)$.
1798	It is an open covering of $X$ and

Stacks 01EO proof consumes exactly the five Form-B / done-brick facts (injective_cech_acyclic,
